title: Photon shot-noise-limited Rydberg-EIT electrometry
authors: Gyeongmin Ju, Ye Jin Yu, Heewoo Kim, Hansol Jeong, Jinhyuk Bae, Changhoon Baek, Han Seb Moon
abstract: Rydberg-atom electrometry is a core technique in the development of highly sensitive quantum electric-field sensors. Its sensitivity based on atom-photon interaction is typically limited by photon shot-noise (PSN) and spectral broadenings. Here, we experimentally demonstrate a near PSN-limited Rydberg electrometry from a 85Rb atomic vapor cell. By engineering atomic coherence through control of residual magnetic fields and laser frequency noise, we achieve the Rydberg electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) with the narrow linewidth of 1.6 MHz, yielding an enhanced spectral slope for high-sensitivity Rydberg-EIT electrometry. Under optimized superheterodyne detection conditions, we obtain an electric-field sensitivity of 12.5(8) nV cm^-1 Hz^-1/2 at 37 GHz, in close agreement with the calculated PSN limit. These results provide direct experimental evidence of the high-sensitive quantum electrometry and establish a practical route toward quantum-noise-limited Rydberg electrometry.
summary: This paper reports on the experimental realization of a highly sensitive electric-field sensor using Rydberg-atom Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT) in a rubidium vapor cell. By meticulously engineering the atomic coherence and employing superheterodyne detection, the authors successfully suppressed technical noise sources. They achieved an electric-field sensitivity matching the fundamental photon shot-noise limit, marking a significant step toward practical quantum-limited sensing.
main_problem: The primary goal is to develop and experimentally demonstrate a highly sensitive quantum electric-field sensor using Rydberg-atom electrometry that approaches the fundamental photon shot-noise limit.
main_result: The authors achieved an electric-field sensitivity of 12.5(8) nV cm^-1 Hz^-1/2, which is in close agreement with the calculated photon shot-noise limit, establishing a practical route for quantum-noise-limited sensing.
method: The experiment utilizes a superheterodyne detection scheme applied to Rydberg Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT) in an atomic vapor cell, requiring active compensation of external magnetic and laser noise.
model_or_system: The system is based on a cascade-type EIT scheme in an 85Rb atomic vapor cell, leveraging the strong, controllable interactions in Rydberg states to enhance the electric-field coupling.
key_observables: Electric-field sensitivity (nV cm^-1 Hz^-1/2), EIT linewidth (achieved 1.6 MHz), and the spectral slope ($\kappa_0$) of the EIT response.
important_parameters: Photon Shot-Noise (PSN) limit, Rydberg-EIT linewidth, and the optimal probe power for maximizing signal-to-noise ratio.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the dominant noise source limiting sensitivity is the probe-photon shot noise, and that the system can be engineered to minimize technical noise sources like magnetic field fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy-level diagram and experimental setup, compare EIT spectra with and without magnetic field compensation (showing linewidth narrowing), and detail the superheterodyne time-domain response and the dependence of sensitivity on probe power.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the basic EIT sensing mechanism, then detailing the necessity and implementation of magnetic field compensation to narrow the linewidth, and finally optimizing the superheterodyne detection scheme to quantify the sensitivity relative to the theoretical PSN limit.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes the boundaries of quantum sensing by experimentally verifying the theoretical limit imposed by quantum noise (shot noise) in a complex atomic system, providing a blueprint for quantum-limited measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2. Effect of magnetic-field compensation on the Rydberg-EIT response. (a) Measured EIT transmission spectra with (red) and without (blue) magnetic-field compensation, showing linewidth narrowing and increased peak transmission. (b) Dependence of the EIT peak amplitude (top) and linewidth (bottom) on the probe laser power, compared with (red) and without (blue) the compensation. The solid lines represent theoretical fits to the experimental data.

Fig. 3. Atomic superheterodyne response of the Rydberg-EIT system. (a) Autler- Townes splitting with a strong LO (blue) and with an additional weak signal field (red). (b) Time-domain probe transmission measured at the operating point in (a), showing a clear oscillation at the LO–signal frequency difference (40 kHz), while the LO-only signal remains stable.

Fig. 4. Electric-field sensitivity and probe-power optimization. (a) Measured electric- field response using AT splitting (red) and superheterodyne detection (blue), showing linear agreement with the calculated field. (b) Extracted slope of the response as a function of probe power. (c) Minimum detectable signal amplitude. (d) Electric-field sensitivity, exhibiting an optimum at 6 μW near the photon shot-noise limit.

Figure 5(b) compares the experimentally measured electric-field sensitivity (blue circles) with the calculated PSN limit (red dashed line), as a function of probe power under the experimental temperature condition. The measured sensitivity follows the same overall trend as the calculated shot-noise limit, confirming that the dominant noise source is consistent with

Fig. 5. Comparison with the photon shot-noise (PSN) limit. (a) Calculated atom number (blue) and PSN-limited sensitivity (red) as a function of vapor-cell temperature. (b) Measured sensitivity (blue) and calculated PSN limit (red dashed line) versus probe power, showing agreement near the optimal operating point. The sensitivity approaches the shot- noise limit near 6 μW (green circle).
